title: Public Sociology and Civil Society: Governance, Politics, and Power By Patricia Mooney Nickel Paradigm Publishers. 2012. 224 pages. $86.70 hardcover
abstract: Public Sociology and Civil Society: Governance, Politics, and Power is a tour de force critique of significant movements in the fields of sociology, civil society studies and public administration, exposing how preeminent ideas (and scholars) in each of these fields, that claim some form of democratic transformation, have rather worked to stabilize current oppressive power relations. It is hardly fair or possible to do justice to the book in this short review and Nickel does not make it easy given the complex argument she is making. Because of its complexity and its far-reaching nature, it is a book that requires a serious but worthwhile investment of thought. The movements or ideas Nickel primarily confronts are no less than Michael Burawoy's notion of public sociology, Lester Salamon's Comparative Nonprofit Sector Project, and Sorrensen and Torfing's network and postliberal governance. All three are related because they are a set of governing practices. In each case, she writes, “an ontological claim is staked by an enterprising advocate,” whose claim is then “institutionalized and encoded in a particular epistemological orientation,” and then the “practice of these institutionalized ontological claims contributed to the regime of knowledge/power that legitimates and stabilizes the contemporary practice of governing” (138).
